name: Pushan
description: Pushan is a significant solar deity in the Hindu pantheon, primarily venerated during the Vedic period. Often depicted as a benevolent guardian, Pushan's domain encompasses journeys, pathways, and the safe passage of travelers, herdsmen, and even souls navigating the afterlife. He is intrinsically linked to prosperity, fertility, and the nourishment of both the physical and spiritual realms. Unlike some other prominent solar deities like Surya, Pushan's role is less about raw power and more about gentle guidance and protection. He is the knower of paths, both literal and metaphorical, guiding individuals towards their intended destinations. He is often invoked in rituals seeking safe travel, successful ventures, and the recovery of lost objects. His connection to cattle and livestock underscores his importance in ensuring the well-being of the agricultural community. Pushan's association with food and nourishment extends to the spiritual realm, as he is believed to guide souls to the afterlife and provide sustenance in the beyond. His golden chariot, pulled by goats or other animals, symbolizes his role as a gentle and accessible guide.
subtitle: Guardian of Pathways

keyMyths.title: Pushan and the Lost Tooth of Shiva
keyMyths.description: During a sacrifice organized by Daksha, Shiva, angered by the exclusion of his wife Sati, retaliated by sending Virabhadra to disrupt the event. In the ensuing chaos, Virabhadra knocked out Pushan's teeth as punishment for eating the sacrificial offerings without reverence. This myth explains Pushan's toothless depiction and highlights the consequences of disrespect towards powerful deities. It also underscores the importance of proper conduct and reverence in religious ceremonies. The myth is often interpreted as a lesson in humility and the recognition of divine authority.

keyMyths.title: Pushan as Guide to the Afterlife
keyMyths.description: Pushan is believed to guide the souls of the deceased to the afterlife. He knows the paths and the dangers that lie ahead, ensuring a safe and smooth transition for the departed. He is invoked in funeral rites to ensure the deceased reaches their final destination without harm. This role as a psychopomp highlights his compassionate nature and his concern for the well-being of individuals even after death. He provides nourishment and protection during this perilous journey, ensuring their safe passage.

keyMyths.title: The Recovery of Lost Objects
keyMyths.description: Pushan is frequently invoked to help find lost objects and strayed cattle. His knowledge of pathways and hidden places makes him an ideal deity to seek assistance from when something valuable is lost. Devotees pray to Pushan, offering him oblations, in the hope that he will guide them to the location of their missing possessions. This aspect of Pushan's mythology underscores his role as a benevolent protector and provider, ensuring the well-being of his devotees in both material and spiritual matters.

sources.author: Jan Gonda
